name: RabbitMQ
scope: A person with INTERMEDIATE proficiency level in RabbitMQ as a competency should be able to explain AMQP 0-9-1 concepts (producers, consumers, brokers, exchanges, queues, bindings, routing keys, vhosts), map business requirements to RabbitMQ use cases, and distinguish it from other messaging systems (Kafka, Redis Streams). They should design and configure exchange types (direct, fanout, topic, headers), queues (durable, exclusive, auto-delete, TTL, lazy, priority), bindings, routing keys, and dead-letter/alternate exchanges, understanding message flow, delivery semantics (at-most/at-least-once), ordering, acknowledgements, publisher confirms, and idempotency. They should know durability and persistence options, storage behavior, high-availability approaches (mirrored/quorum queues), dead-lettering, retry/backoff strategies, and common messaging patterns (work queues, pub/sub, request/reply, delayed messages). They should securely configure users, vhosts, permissions, TLS, and client settings, and use at least one client library (Java, .NET, Python, Node.js, Go) or framework (Spring AMQP, MassTransit, NServiceBus), handling connections, channels, serialization, and configuration-driven integration. They should install/configure RabbitMQ (standalone or cluster), manage plugins, networking, resource limits, and collaborate with DevOps for sizing, backup, and containerized deployments (Docker, Helm, Kubernetes). They should use management UI, HTTP API, CLI tools (rabbitmqctl, rabbitmq-diagnostics), interpret metrics, troubleshoot issues, and define monitoring/alerting. They should design and implement tests (unit, integration, contract), automate topology/configuration deployment, and validate resilience and observability. They should document topologies, naming, routing, error handling, and message contracts, communicate designs and trade-offs, participate in reviews and post-mortems, and mentor juniors on best practices and common pitfalls.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Conceptual Foundations & Use Cases","scope_included":"- Understand AMQP 0-9-1 concepts as implemented by RabbitMQ (producers, consumers, brokers, exchanges, queues, bindings, routing keys, virtual hosts)
- Distinguish RabbitMQ’s role versus other messaging paradigms (pub/sub, point-to-point, RPC, streaming) and tools (e.g., Kafka, Redis Streams) and pick RabbitMQ when appropriate
- Map business and system requirements (latency, throughput, reliability, ordering, fan-out needs) to RabbitMQ capabilities
- Recognize when to use RabbitMQ for:
  - Asynchronous processing and background jobs
  - Decoupling services and microservices communication
  - Event-driven architectures and integration between heterogeneous systems
  - Work queues and load distribution between workers
  - Request/Reply and RPC-style messaging (with awareness of trade-offs)
- Identify when RabbitMQ is not a good fit (extreme event streaming/analytics workloads, very large message payloads, strict global ordering, long-term event storage)
- Understand core terminology around:
  - Message metadata (headers, properties, content-type, correlation-id, reply-to, expiration, priority)
  - Delivery semantics (at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once *as an approximation*)
  - Synchronous vs asynchronous communication patterns using RabbitMQ","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"RabbitMQ Architecture & Messaging Model","scope_included":"- Explain RabbitMQ’s logical architecture:
  - Broker, virtual hosts, connections, channels, exchanges, queues, bindings
  - Connection vs channel lifecycle and resource usage
- Understand single-node versus clustered RabbitMQ at a conceptual level:
  - Purpose of clustering (high availability, scalability, single logical broker)
  - Basic understanding of mirrored/HA queues and quorum queues concepts (without needing deep tuning expertise)
- Describe how messages flow through the broker:
  - From producer to exchange, through routing to queues, to consumers
  - How acknowledgements and redeliveries feed back into the flow
- Understand logical isolation with virtual hosts and its impact on:
  - Security boundaries
  - Multi-tenancy
  - Resource and naming isolation
- Understand basic plugin architecture conceptually (management plugin, shovel, federation, delayed message, etc.) and know how they extend core functionality
- Know RabbitMQ limitations and architectural constraints (e.g., per-queue throughput characteristics, connection and channel limits, memory vs disk usage patterns) at a conceptual level","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Exchanges, Queues, Bindings & Routing Mechanics","scope_included":"- Understand all core exchange types and when to use them:
  - Direct exchanges and exact routing
  - Fanout exchanges for broadcast/fan-out scenarios
  - Topic exchanges for pattern-based routing (wildcards * and #)
  - Headers exchanges conceptually and why they are less commonly used
- Design queue and exchange topologies for typical use cases:
  - One queue per consumer group vs per consumer instance
  - Shared queues for work-stealing vs dedicated queues for isolation
  - Multi-tenant routing strategies (e.g., routing keys that encode tenant or region)
- Use bindings effectively:
  - Configure routing keys and binding keys for topic/direct exchanges
  - Understand implications of many bindings on routing performance
- Understand and apply queue-level behaviors:
  - Exclusive vs non-exclusive queues
  - Auto-delete, auto-expire, and TTL (per-queue and per-message)
  - Lazy queues conceptually and when they might be useful
  - Message priority queues and their operational implications
- Use alternate exchanges and dead-letter exchanges conceptually to handle unroutable and failed messages
- Understand implications of queue length, message size, and prefetch on memory and IO usage
- Know how to design routing keys and binding conventions for maintainable, extensible routing (e.g., dot-separated topic hierarchies)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Message Flow, Ordering, Acknowledgements & Delivery Guarantees","scope_included":"- Understand message delivery semantics supported by RabbitMQ:
  - At-most-once vs at-least-once delivery
  - Why exactly-once is not natively guaranteed and how to approximate it at application level (idempotency, deduplication)
- Use acknowledgement modes appropriately:
  - Manual acks vs auto-acks (no-ack) and trade-offs
  - Negative acknowledgements (nack) and rejecting messages with/without requeue
  - Understand redelivery semantics and redelivered flag
- Apply consumer prefetch and QoS settings to control message flow and balance throughput vs fairness
- Understand message ordering in RabbitMQ:
  - Ordering guarantees within a queue and impact of multiple consumers
  - Effects of redelivery, requeueing, and parallel consumers on perceived ordering
- Implement consumer logic that is safe under at-least-once semantics:
  - Idempotent processing
  - Handling duplicates and redeliveries
  - Avoiding data loss when using auto-ack
- Understand and apply publisher confirms conceptually:
  - Difference between transactional publishing and publisher confirms
  - Per-message vs batch confirms (at a conceptual level)
- Recognize and mitigate common message loss scenarios:
  - Not using confirms for important messages
  - Acking before processing is complete
  - Using auto-delete/auto-ack incorrectly","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Durability, Persistence & Reliability Features","scope_included":"- Understand and configure durability options correctly:
  - Durable vs transient exchanges and queues
  - Persistent vs non-persistent messages
  - Relationship between durability and broker restarts
- Use message persistence appropriately for different workloads:
  - When persistence is necessary vs when in-memory/transient is acceptable
  - Impact of persistence on performance and disk IO
- Understand RabbitMQ’s storage behavior conceptually:
  - How messages move between memory and disk
  - Impact of large backlogs and lazy queues
- Design for resilience against broker restarts and common failures:
  - Ensuring critical topology (exchanges, queues, bindings) is declared on startup by applications
  - Using policies to enforce durability or HA settings
- Configure and reason about dead-lettering behaviors:
  - Dead-letter exchanges (DLX) and dead-letter queues (DLQ)
  - Using TTL/expiration, max-length, and reject/nack behaviors to drive DLX
- Know the basics of high-availability approaches at a conceptual level:
  - Classic mirrored queues vs quorum queues (when to prefer each, high-level characteristics)
  - Trade-offs between availability, consistency, and performance
- Plan retry strategies with backoff using RabbitMQ mechanisms (DLX with TTL, delayed message patterns) rather than naive tight retry loops","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Messaging Patterns & Integration Scenarios","scope_included":"- Design and implement common messaging patterns with RabbitMQ:
  - Work queues / competing consumers
  - Publish/subscribe event distribution
  - Topic-based routing for multi-tenant or multi-service events
  - Request/Reply (RPC-like) using correlation-id and reply-to
  - Dead-letter queues for failure isolation
  - Delayed or scheduled messages using TTL + DLX or plugins (conceptually)
- Integrate RabbitMQ with microservices and legacy systems:
  - Use appropriate message formats (JSON, Avro, protobuf, etc.) and understand their impact on compatibility
  - Use correlation IDs and message headers for tracing and observability across services
- Understand and apply idempotency and deduplication strategies at the application layer
- Reason about transactional boundaries:
  - Coordinating message publishing with database updates (outbox patterns, eventual consistency concepts)
  - Avoiding distributed transactions when possible
- Support side-effect isolation and failure handling in workflows:
  - Compensating actions when messages fail after partial processing
  - Breaking down long-running operations into multiple messages/steps
- Understand basic saga/choreography approaches where RabbitMQ is the event bus (conceptually, not necessarily design of complex sagas)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Basic Performance, Scaling & Topology Design","scope_included":"- Understand the main levers that affect RabbitMQ performance:
  - Message size and payload format
  - Number of queues and bindings
  - Number of connections and channels
  - Prefetch/QoS and consumer concurrency
  - Use of confirms and persistence
- Design a topology that balances throughput, reliability, and maintainability:
  - Appropriate number of queues per use case
  - Reasonable fan-out and binding counts
  - Separation of concerns using multiple exchanges and routing keys
- Tune client-side parameters for typical workloads:
  - Consumer prefetch values for fair load distribution and throughput
  - Batch acks and batch publishing strategies at a conceptual level
- Reason about horizontal scaling strategies conceptually:
  - Adding consumers versus adding queues
  - Scaling producers and the impact on the broker
- Recognize signs of performance or capacity issues from application perspective:
  - Growing queue backlogs
  - Increased redeliveries and timeouts
  - Consumer lag relative to incoming rate
- Choose between simple and more advanced queue types conceptually for scaling and reliability (e.g., classic vs quorum queues) without needing deep cluster internals knowledge","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Security, Tenancy & Access Control","scope_included":"- Understand RabbitMQ’s security model at an intermediate level:
  - Users, passwords, and built-in authentication
  - Virtual hosts (vhosts) as security and isolation boundaries
  - Permissions (configure, write, read) and their impact on what clients can do
- Apply best practices for securing applications using RabbitMQ:
  - Use separate users and vhosts per application or trust boundary
  - Principle of least privilege for permissions
  - Avoid sharing credentials across unrelated services
- Understand TLS/SSL usage conceptually:
  - Why TLS is needed and what problems it solves (confidentiality, integrity)
  - Difference between AMQP port and AMQPS/SSL port at a high level
- Use secure client connection settings from applications:
  - Pass credentials securely (env vars, secrets management)
  - Configure connection parameters (host, port, vhost, heartbeat, frame max) appropriately
- Understand basic auditing and compliance needs:
  - Awareness of logging of connections, authentication failures, and authorization errors
  - Recognize when to involve security specialists for advanced topics (mutual TLS, external auth backends like LDAP/OAuth)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Error Handling, Dead-Lettering & Recovery Strategies","scope_included":"- Implement robust error handling in consumers:
  - Distinguish between transient and permanent errors
  - Decide when to requeue vs dead-letter vs drop
  - Avoid tight retry loops that overload the system
- Configure dead-lettering behavior effectively:
  - DLX and DLQ configuration via queue arguments and policies
  - Use message TTLs and queue length limits to trigger dead-lettering
- Design retry and backoff strategies using RabbitMQ features:
  - Delayed retries using TTL + DLX patterns or delayed message plugins
  - Fixed and exponential backoff at the application level
- Understand and handle poison messages:
  - Identify messages that always fail
  - Route them to dead-letter queues and build processes to inspect and remediate
- Implement recovery from typical failure modes:
  - Consumer reconnect logic on connection/channel failures
  - Safe recovery after consumer crashes (reprocessing redelivered messages)
- Build basic operational runbooks or procedures (conceptual) for:
  - Investigating dead-letter queues
  - Clearing or requeueing messages safely
  - Temporarily stopping/starting consumers for maintenance","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Client Libraries & Application Integration","scope_included":"- Use at least one RabbitMQ client library fluently (e.g., Java amqp-client or Spring AMQP, .NET clients, Node.js amqplib, Python pika, Go libraries) and understand how concepts map to other languages
- Manage connections and channels correctly:
  - Reuse connections and channels appropriately (not creating per-message connections)
  - Handle connection loss, automatic recovery/reconnect behavior
- Implement producers and consumers with:
  - Durable topologies (declaring durable exchanges and queues from code)
  - Proper use of acknowledgements
  - Proper handling of connection/channel exceptions
- Use higher-level frameworks where appropriate (e.g., Spring AMQP, Spring Cloud Stream, MassTransit, NServiceBus) and understand:
  - How they map to RabbitMQ primitives
  - Their configuration of queues, exchanges, routing keys
- Handle message serialization and deserialization robustly:
  - Enforce schemas or contracts where appropriate
  - Manage versioning and backward compatibility (e.g., optional fields)
- Implement configuration-driven messaging behavior:
  - Externalize connection and topology settings (URIs, vhosts, credentials, queue/exchange names)
  - Support different environments (dev, test, prod) without code changes","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Installation, Configuration & Environment Management","scope_included":"- Perform basic RabbitMQ installation and configuration (with or without automation tools):
  - Install RabbitMQ and Erlang on Linux/Windows/macOS servers or containers
  - Enable and configure core plugins (management UI, Prometheus, etc.)
- Configure broker-level settings relevant to application teams:
  - Configure vhosts, users, and permissions for applications
  - Set up sensible defaults for resource limits (max connections, channels where applicable)
- Understand configuration mechanisms:
  - rabbitmq.conf / advanced.config basics
  - Environment variables for containerized deployments
- Participate in designing simple cluster setups (conceptually, with ops/SRE support):
  - Joining nodes into a cluster (high-level understanding)
  - Awareness of mirrored/quorum queue configuration via policies
- Configure networking aspects relevant to apps:
  - Ports and firewalls
  - Load balancer considerations for RabbitMQ
- Collaborate with DevOps/SRE to:
  - Size environments (CPU, memory, disk) based on expected workloads
  - Plan basic backup and restore approaches (conceptually)
- Use Docker images or charts (Helm, Kubernetes operators) at a practical level:
  - Understand common configuration options for container-based RabbitMQ
  - Provide appropriate values for application-centric needs (vhosts, users, limits)","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Operations, Monitoring & Troubleshooting Tooling","scope_included":"- Use the RabbitMQ Management UI and HTTP API effectively to:
  - Inspect connections, channels, exchanges, queues, and consumers
  - View message rates, queue lengths, and basic performance metrics
  - Create, modify, and delete queues, exchanges, bindings, users, and vhosts (within allowed permissions)
- Use core CLI tools in collaboration with ops/SRE:
  - rabbitmqctl for status checks, listing queues, connections
  - rabbitmq-diagnostics for health checks and basic diagnostics (conceptual understanding)
  - rabbitmq-plugins for enabling/disabling plugins
- Interpret key health and performance indicators:
  - Queue depth trends, publish and consume rates
  - Connection and channel counts
  - Memory and disk alarms conceptually
- Work with monitoring and alerting tools:
  - Understand RabbitMQ metrics exported to Prometheus, Graphite, Cloud-specific monitoring
  - Help define basic alerts (e.g., queue backlog, connection spikes, failed connections)
- Troubleshoot common issues from an application-centric viewpoint:
  - Connection failures and authentication/authorization errors
  - Consumers not receiving messages due to bindings or routing misconfigurations
  - Stuck or growing queues, dead-letter queues filling up
- Participate in incident resolution:
  - Provide message flow and topology insight during outages
  - Help test fixes and verify message flow restoration
  - Support safe draining, moving, or purging of queues under guidance","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Testing, CI/CD & Quality Practices for RabbitMQ-Integrated Systems","scope_included":"- Design and implement tests for message-based integrations:
  - Unit tests that isolate producer/consumer logic
  - Integration tests with in-memory or containerized RabbitMQ
  - Contract tests between services that communicate via RabbitMQ
- Use tools and approaches for local testing:
  - Dockerized RabbitMQ for local development and CI pipelines
  - Test containers / embedded brokers where applicable
- Validate topology and configuration as part of CI/CD:
  - Ensure exchanges, queues, bindings, and policies are created as expected in non-production environments
  - Fail builds when incompatible topology or contract changes are introduced
- Test resilience behaviors:
  - Simulate connection drops, consumer crashes, and message failures
  - Verify retry, backoff, and dead-letter behavior
- Automate deployment of RabbitMQ-related configuration:
  - Declarative configuration for queues, exchanges, bindings via code or infrastructure-as-code (e.g., Terraform, Ansible, Helm)
  - Keep application and broker configuration in sync across environments
- Ensure observability aspects are covered in tests:
  - Verify that correlation IDs and trace headers propagate through RabbitMQ messages
  - Validate logging/metrics behavior for message processing","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Collaboration, Documentation & Design Communication","scope_included":"- Document RabbitMQ usage and topology for the team:
  - Exchange and queue naming conventions
  - Routing key patterns and semantics
  - Dead-letter and retry strategies
  - Expected message schemas and contracts
- Collaborate with architects and other teams to:
  - Review and refine messaging-based designs
  - Identify integration points and define clear responsibilities between services
- Communicate RabbitMQ characteristics to non-experts:
  - Explain trade-offs of asynchronous messaging vs synchronous APIs
  - Set expectations around delivery guarantees and failure modes
- Maintain and evolve documentation as systems grow:
  - Update diagrams and docs when new queues/exchanges/patterns are introduced
  - Deprecate and remove unused queues and routing paths in a controlled way
- Participate in incident post-mortems and design reviews:
  - Contribute analysis of RabbitMQ’s role in issues/outages
  - Suggest design or configuration improvements for resilience and clarity
- Mentor junior engineers on RabbitMQ basics:
  - Help them understand key concepts and best practices
  - Review their code and configurations for common pitfalls","is_non_core_section":true}]}

name: Python - FastAPI
scope: A person with INTERMEDIATE proficiency level in Python FastAPI as a competency should be able to / is capable of planning, structuring, and maintaining moderately complex APIs that use routers for route organization and advanced data modeling/validation with Pydantic’s validators and custom data types. They handle async/await patterns, concurrency, and performance tuning, recognizing blocking operations or bottlenecks and offloading secondary tasks with background jobs. They design secure endpoints through solid authentication/authorization techniques (OAuth2, role-based access), implement best practices for token handling, and appreciate further security concerns (CSRF, XSS). They manage database interactions through SQLAlchemy or equivalent ORMs, ensuring transactional integrity, handling schema migrations, and optimizing queries when needed. They write robust tests and integrate Pytest or similar frameworks with FastAPI-specific dependency overrides, ensuring maintainable and isolated testing. They containerize and deploy applications with Docker, manage environment-based configurations, and incorporate structured logging plus monitoring (Prometheus, Grafana) for production readiness. They centralize error management with custom exception handlers and consistent error payloads, employing protective patterns like retries, rate-limiting, or circuit breakers, and handle advanced middleware usage. They produce usable OpenAPI-based interactive documentation, refining schema tags and metadata as the codebase evolves. They understand message queue integrations (RabbitMQ, Kafka), caching with Redis, and basic microservice or SOA concepts, though not at a deep architectural level. They collaborate effectively in code reviews, mentor junior developers, maintain consistent code quality, and prepare to step into deeper concurrency, scalability, and performance exploration toward advanced levels.
long_scope:
At the INTERMEDIATE level (typically around 3–5 years of focused experience in Python FastAPI), practitioners are expected to have moved beyond simply “getting things to work” and are now able to design, implement, and maintain moderately complex FastAPI applications with sound structure, testing rigor, and maintainable code quality. While still building on the strong foundations of Python fluency and core FastAPI concepts, an INTERMEDIATE-level scope covers greater depth in architecture, concurrency, security, and broader ecosystem usage than BASIC or BEGINNER levels. Below is a comprehensive view of what belongs in the scope of an INTERMEDIATE FastAPI practitioner’s competencies.

1. Core Application and Service Design
• Strong Familiarity with FastAPI Application Structure: The individual can lay out application packages or modules, use routers to organize routes logically, and apply dependency injection patterns to keep code clean and reusable.  
• Clear Understanding of the Request-Response Cycle: They can articulate how FastAPI processes incoming requests, resolves dependencies, handles exceptions, and returns responses.  
• Maintenance of Modular and Extensible Code: They incorporate best practices around directory structure, configuration management, and environment-based settings, ensuring that the codebase is scalable and less prone to tight coupling.  

2. Advanced Handling of Routes, Models, and Validation
• Usage of Pydantic for Data Modeling and Validation: The individual has in-depth comfort defining models using Pydantic, understands how to leverage validators, custom data types, and schema configurations, and knows how to handle common data validation issues (e.g., required fields, custom constraints).  
• Efficient Router Utilization: Strategies for grouping related endpoints, versioning APIs, and seamlessly integrating multiple routers for complex applications are part of their standard approach.  

3. Concurrency, Asynchronous Patterns, and Performance
• Asynchronous Programming Proficiency: They know when and why to use async/await in FastAPI, can reason about the concurrency model, and can manage pitfalls of blocking I/O within async routes or background tasks.  
• Managing Background Workflows: They incorporate or design background tasks for non-user-facing operations or for tasks that can be offloaded outside the main request-response flow (e.g., sending emails, performing batch requests).  
• Recognizing and Addressing Performance Bottlenecks: They monitor and optimize aspects of an API that might degrade performance, such as inefficient database queries, unbounded concurrency, or synchronous calls that block the event loop.  

4. Security, Authentication, and Authorization
• Intermediate Authentication Techniques: The individual is familiar with multiple authentication flows (e.g., OAuth2 with PasswordBearer), can handle token storage and refresh mechanisms responsibly, and sets up secure endpoints.  
• Authorization Strategies: They are able to define and enforce roles/permissions effectively, setting up an appropriate structure to manage user roles in application routes.  
• Deeper Appreciation for Common Security Concerns: They can implement solutions to challenges like CSRF, rate-limiting, or other security best practices necessary in a production environment.  

5. Database Integration and Data Persistence
• Confident Use of ORMs or Database Libraries: They can integrate SQLAlchemy or equivalent libraries for relational databases, manage migrations, and design moderately complex schema relationships.  
• Handling Transactions and Data Integrity: They ensure critical operations happen within controlled transactions, maintain data consistency, and set up error-handling logic around database commits/rollbacks.  
• Mastery Over Basic Query Optimization: They can evaluate query plans for simpler performance improvements, use indexes appropriately, and avoid N+1 query problems in typical use cases.  

6. Robust Testing and Quality Assurance
• Familiarity with Testing Frameworks and Approaches: They use Pytest or an equivalent testing library to write unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests for FastAPI applications.  
• Dependency Injection for Testability: By leveraging FastAPI’s dependency injection patterns properly, they can mock or override dependencies in test scenarios, ensuring well-isolated and reliable tests.  
• CI/CD Alignment: They integrate test suites into a continuous integration pipeline, ensuring stable and consistent test outcomes before application deployment.  

7. Configuration, Deployment, and Observability
• Environment-Specific Configuration: They structure the application to handle multiple environments (development, staging, production), using environment variables or configuration files in a secure manner.  
• Containerization and Deployment Fundamentals: They are aware of Docker containerization best practices, can produce optimized Dockerfiles for FastAPI, and understand deployment workflows (e.g., using Kubernetes or serverless platforms).  
• Logging and Monitoring: They integrate structured logging (e.g., with Python’s standard logging or third-party loggers) and can set up metrics or monitoring dashboards (e.g., Prometheus, Grafana, or equivalent) to observe application health and performance.  

8. Error Handling, Exception Management, and Resilience
• Standardized Error Responses: They define clear error-handling strategies—custom exception handlers, robust error models for consistent error messages, and structured JSON error payloads—making the service predictable for clients.  
• Retries and Circuit-Breaking in Distributed Systems: While not always mandatory at this level, they can reasonably explain or set up basic resiliency patterns that protect their service from external failures.  

9. API Documentation and Schema Management
• Knowledge of OpenAPI Schemas via FastAPI: The individual can ensure their API endpoints are well-documented, customizing schema generation, tags, and metadata to produce user-friendly documentation.  
• Implementation of Interactive Documentation: They understand how FastAPI automatically generates interactive documentation (Swagger UI, ReDoc) and keep the documentation current with minimal overhead.  

10. Cross-Cutting Concerns and Broader Ecosystem Patterns
• Middleware Usage and Creation: They can configure standard middlewares (e.g., CORS, GZip) and know how to create custom middlewares to handle cross-cutting functionality (logging, metrics, etc.).  
• Handling Advanced Configurations or Integrations: Adapters for message queues (e.g., RabbitMQ, Kafka) or caching systems (e.g., Redis) can be integrated at a moderate level, with a grasp of how concurrency and data consistency are affected.  
• Familiarity with Common Extension Libraries: The individual can integrate widely used libraries (e.g., JWT libraries for authentication, scheduling libraries for periodic background tasks) with minimal friction, and maintain these integrations in production.  

11. Team Collaboration, Code Reviews, and Knowledge Sharing
• Code Review and Team Alignment: They can participate in constructive code reviews, identify performance, security, or design issues, and offer helpful feedback following established standards.  
• Documentation and Knowledge Handoff: They maintain readable docstrings, guides, or wiki pages that describe architectural decisions, build steps, and deployment/run instructions for team members.  
• Mentoring Junior Colleagues: Although not the main focus, they can guide more junior team members through fundamental FastAPI and Python issues, fostering a culture of continuous learning.  

12. Evolving Skills in Preparation for Advanced Levels
• Deeper Concurrency and Scalability Practice: They have begun experimenting with advanced concurrency patterns (e.g., concurrency with external resources, websockets) but are not yet experts.  
• Preliminary Microservices or Service-Oriented Architecture Exposure: They can discuss how to break monolithic APIs into microservices when appropriate, though the design might not be as refined as someone at the ADVANCED or EXPERT level.  
• Preparing for Production Maturity: They are aware of additional steps needed for secure, scalable production readiness (e.g., advanced load balancing, event-driven architecture, custom performance metrics), even if they do not own all aspects of these.

Overall, this level of scope for Python FastAPI INTERMEDIATE proficiency ensures that the developer can handle real-world complexity while adhering to best practices in API design, security, and maintainability. They have surpassed merely getting endpoints working and now demonstrate the ability to structure, validate, secure, test, deploy, and maintain services with confidence within a team. They are aware of common pitfalls—both in asynchronous Python programming and in distributed systems contexts—and have strategies to address them. This puts them in a position to evolve towards ADVANCED proficiency, where deeper specialization, optimization, and breadth of knowledge around performance tuning, architecture, and ecosystem tooling will be expected.
